title: Sibling Rivalry as Adult Resentment
dek: *The fury you never voiced at age seven doesn't vanish—it calcifies into adult coldness, sarcasm, and strategic distance. When your brother's name appears on your phone, that tightness in your chest is old information trying to surface. This is the anatomy of sibling rivalry that never got its proper funeral, and the somatic work that finally lets you put it down.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Adult sibling resentment often masks unprocessed childhood anger that was too dangerous to express when you were small. Your body still carries the physiological signature of those early injustices, even when your conscious mind has moved on. Healing requires naming the original wound, feeling the anger in your tissues, and choosing whether to repair or release the relationship.

She's driving home from Thanksgiving dinner when her hands start to ache. Not the dull throb of arthritis or the sharp complaint of carpal tunnel, but a specific pressure in her palms, as if she's been gripping something invisible for hours. She has. Her younger sister spent the entire meal recounting her recent promotion, her remodeled kitchen, the school her kids got into, while their mother nodded and beamed. No one asked her about the manuscript she just finished, the one she's been working on for three years. No one ever does. She tells herself it doesn't matter. She tells herself she's happy for her sister. But her hands know better. They remember being nine years old, watching her sister get the bedroom with the bay window, the one she'd been promised first. They remember learning that wanting things out loud made you selfish, that good girls swallowed their protests. Her hands are still holding that swallow, thirty-two years later. ## The Ghost Arithmetic of Fairness You were born into a calculus you didn't design. Every family operates on an invisible ledger of attention, praise, resources, and favor, and children are merciless accountants. You knew, before you had language for it, whether the distribution was equitable. You knew who got more. Who got better. Who got believed. And if you were the one who got less, your nervous system recorded each instance as a small betrayal, a micro-dose of injustice that your developing brain couldn't yet process as grief. Sibling rivalry in childhood is developmentally normal, even healthy when parents hold it well. But when the imbalance is chronic, when one child is consistently positioned as the golden one and another as the problem, the disappointment, or the invisible one, something hardens. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby that needs to be held with tenderness, not shamed or stuffed away. But most families don't have room for a child's rage at their sibling. So you learned to smile. To be the mature one. To stop keeping score out loud while your body kept perfect records in tissue and breath. The resentment you carry now isn't pettiness. It's an archive of unwitnessed hurt, and it lives in your shoulders, your jaw, your gut. In my own practice, I've watched clients uncover decade-old sibling wounds that still dictate how they move through the world - who they trust, how much space they allow themselves to take up, whether they believe their needs matter. ## When the Body Remembers What the Mind Excuses Your conscious mind is skilled at rationalization. *It was a long time ago. They were just kids. Mom and Dad did their best.* But the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system makes it clear that unprocessed anger doesn't evaporate through cognitive reframing alone. It encodes itself in your nervous system as a persistent low-grade activation, a subtle readiness for threat that colors every interaction with the person who wronged you. This is why you can be forty-five years old, financially secure, loved by good people, and still feel your stomach clench when your sibling's name appears in a group text. The anger isn't about the present slight. It's about the accumulated weight of being unseen, undefended, or unfavored when you were too small to leave. Your body is still braced for the next inequity, still waiting for someone to notice that you mattered too. Beverly Engel, in her work on emotional abuse, emphasizes that children who grow up feeling chronically less-than often develop a kind of hypervigilance around fairness in all their relationships. You might find yourself tallying who texted last, who remembered your birthday, who offered help when you needed it. This isn't neurosis. It's your system trying to ensure you never again become the one who gives everything and receives nothing. The old wound is asking: *Am I worth as much as they are?* ## The Specific Shapes Resentment Takes Adult sibling resentment rarely announces itself as rage. It's subtler. You return calls three days late. You're polite but never warm. You attend family events but leave early. You feel a private satisfaction when their life hits turbulence, then feel ashamed of that satisfaction. You compare your children to theirs and either gloat or despair. You've built an entire architecture of distance that looks like civility but feels, in your body, like a locked door. Some people freeze their siblings out entirely, a cutoff that family systems therapists recognize as a form of self-protection when repair feels impossible. Others stay engaged but numb, going through motions that lack any genuine warmth. And some oscillate, reaching out in hope and then withdrawing in disappointment when the old patterns reassert themselves. All of these responses make sense. They're strategies you developed to survive an emotional environment that didn't have space for your full humanity. Karla McLaren's work on emotional literacy reminds us that resentment is actually a compound emotion, a mixture of anger and envy and grief that we've compressed into a single, manageable feeling. When you peel it apart, you often find underneath it a child's bewildered question: *Why wasn't I enough?* That question deserves an answer, even if the answer is simply: *You were always enough. The failure was never yours.* ## What Your Throat Knows Notice, right now, what happens in your body when you picture your sibling's face. Do you feel tightness across your collarbones? A pulling sensation in your solar plexus? A sudden urge to swallow or clear your throat? These are the places where unsaid words collect. Your throat, in particular, holds the history of every sentence you edited, every protest you swallowed, every time you said "it's fine" when it wasn't. In our experience, the clients who've made the most progress with sibling resentment are the ones who've learned to track sensation first, story second. The narrative you tell yourself about why you're angry can actually become a defense against feeling the anger itself. But your throat doesn't lie. Your jaw doesn't rationalize. When you drop below the storyline and simply notice the pressure, the heat, the tightness, you're meeting the anger in its original, pre-verbal form. This is where transformation starts. Try this now: place one hand on your throat and one on your belly. Breathe slowly and picture a specific moment from childhood when you felt overlooked in favor of your sibling. Don't try to change the feeling. Just notice where it lives. Is there contraction? Heat? A sense of something rising or sinking? Stay with the sensation for three breaths. This is you beginning to befriend the anger your younger self couldn't express. [Waking the Tiger - Peter Levine](https://www.amazon.com/dp/155643943X?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a profound map for working with these frozen survival responses, showing how sensation-tracking can complete what was left unfinished in your nervous system. ## The Myth of Closure and the Reality of Grief You might be hoping this article will tell you how to fix the relationship. How to finally make your sibling see what they did. How to extract the apology you've been waiting for. I won't lie to you: that might never happen. Some siblings lack the self-awareness or emotional courage to acknowledge the imbalance. Some are still so embedded in the family myth - the one where they're the hero and you're the supporting character - that they genuinely can't see your pain. And some simply don't care. This is where grief enters. Not the performative grief of a funeral, but the private, bone-deep grief of accepting that the sibling relationship you needed will never materialize. That the repair you longed for may not be possible. That you might have to build a good life anyway, without their recognition or remorse. Over the years I've seen clients resist this grief ferociously, because grieving feels like giving up. But it's actually the opposite. Grieving is how you stop waiting. It's how you retrieve the energy you've been spending on hope that hasn't borne fruit, and redirect it toward your own healing. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that anger, when it's allowed to move, transforms into clarity. You don't have to forgive. You don't have to reconcile. But you do have to stop pretending the wound doesn't exist. [Rage Becomes Her - Soraya Chemaly](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1501189557?tag=spankyspinola-20) names this precisely: women especially are taught to perform harmony at the expense of their own fury, and that performance costs them their vitality. Your resentment is information. It's telling you what mattered, what still matters, and where your boundaries need to be. ## A Five-Minute Practice: The Unsent Letter Set a timer for five minutes. On a piece of paper or a blank document, write a letter to your sibling that you will never send. Don't edit. Don't soften. Don't worry about being fair or kind. Write what you actually feel: the anger, the envy, the sadness, the longing, the contempt. Let yourself be as petty, as hurt, as furious as you actually are. This isn't about them. It's about giving your internal experience a voice. When the timer goes off, pause. Notice what's happening in your body. Is there relief? More anger? Tears? Whatever's there is right. You don't have to do anything with this letter. You can burn it, shred it, or save it in a folder you never open. The point isn't the letter. It's the act of letting the truth move from your body onto the page. This small practice, repeated whenever the resentment spikes, can prevent the anger from calcifying further. It gives your system proof that the feelings are allowed, that they won't destroy you, that you can hold them without collapsing or exploding. ## When to Repair and When to Release Some sibling relationships can be rebuilt. If both people are willing to acknowledge harm, sit with discomfort, and commit to a different pattern, repair is possible. It requires that your sibling can hear your anger without defensiveness, that they can sit in the reality of having hurt you even if they didn't mean to. It requires that you can express your needs without demanding that they retroactively become the sibling you needed in childhood. That person is gone. The question is whether the adults you both are now can build something new. But not every relationship is repairable, and that's okay. Sometimes the healthiest choice is a boundary that looks like distance. You can release someone with love - or at least without active malice - and still choose not to let them close. You can show up for aging parents without pretending you and your sibling are close. You can send a birthday text and mean it, even if you never call. [The fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) on this site offers specific practices for working with anger that's too hot to hold safely, and those tools apply just as much to sibling resentment as they do to any other relational fire. In Chinese medicine, unresolved anger injures the liver, which governs the smooth flow of qi throughout the body. When anger stagnates, you might notice irritability, insomnia, headaches, or a feeling of being stuck in multiple areas of life. [Plum Flower Suan Zao Ren Tang (200 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00ML3HQ7Y?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a classical formula that nourishes the heart and calms the spirit when emotional strain has disrupted sleep, though it's not a substitute for the deeper work of processing what you feel. [Our herbal cabinet](/herbs) includes other formulas that support the nervous system during emotionally intense periods, but the herbs are adjuncts. The real medicine is your willingness to feel what you've been avoiding. ## What You Owe Yourself You don't owe your sibling forgiveness. You don't owe your parents a performance of sibling harmony. You don't owe anyone access to you if that access costs you your peace. What you owe yourself is honesty. You owe yourself the dignity of acknowledging that what happened mattered, that it hurt, and that the hurt didn't make you weak or petty or bad. You owe yourself the chance to stop carrying resentment like a stone in your pocket, pulling it out to turn over whenever you're alone. This doesn't mean the anger disappears. It means you stop being afraid of it. It means you learn to meet it with curiosity instead of shame. *Oh, there you are again. What are you trying to tell me?* The anger is a messenger. It's trying to restore your sense of worth, to remind you that you deserved better, to insist that your needs are legitimate. When you listen to it without letting it run your life, it can actually become a source of clarity and power. If you're drawn to explore the spiritual dimensions of anger work, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers reflections on how rage and grief intersect with the sacred, how the feelings we've been taught to exile can become doorways to deeper self-knowing. And [the assessments hub](/assessments) includes tools for tracking your own emotional patterns, so you can see where the resentment spikes and what specific triggers reactivate the old wound. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it normal to still be angry at a sibling decades later? Yes. Entirely normal. The intensity of sibling bonds means that early injuries cut deep, and if those injuries were never acknowledged or repaired, the anger doesn't have anywhere to go. It's not a sign that you're stuck or immature. It's a sign that something real happened, and your body hasn't forgotten. The question isn't whether the anger is normal, but whether it's still serving you or whether it's time to process it in a new way. ### How do I know if I should try to talk to my sibling about this or just let it go? Ask yourself: do I believe they have the capacity to hear me? Have they shown any willingness to acknowledge harm in other relationships? Do I want repair, or do I want vindication? If you genuinely want to rebuild the relationship and you have evidence that they can engage with difficult conversations, a conversation might be worth attempting. But if you're hoping the conversation will make them finally see your worth, that's a setup for more disappointment. Sometimes letting go means stopping the pursuit of recognition from someone who's proven they can't offer it. ### What if my sibling is still the favorite and it's affecting my relationship with my parents? This is one of the most painful dimensions of sibling resentment, the way it triangulates your entire family system. You might need to grieve the parents you wish you'd had, the ones who would have seen the inequity and corrected it. And you might need to build a relationship with your parents that's separate from your sibling, one that doesn't require you to compete or compare. Some people find that limiting contact with the whole family system is the only way to stop reinjuring themselves. That's a legitimate choice, and it doesn't make you the bad one for setting the boundary. ## The Long View and the Tender Present Here's what I want you to know: the fact that you're reading this article means you're already doing the work. You're naming what you feel instead of numbing it. You're looking for language and tools instead of pretending the resentment doesn't exist. That matters. That's courage, even if it doesn't feel like it yet. The path forward isn't linear. Some days you'll feel clear and free. Other days the resentment will rise up sharp and fresh, as if no time has passed. Both are part of the process. Healing isn't the absence of anger. It's the ability to feel the anger fully, let it move through you, and still choose how you want to live. It's the difference between being controlled by the old wound and being informed by it. You get to decide what kind of relationship, if any, you want with your sibling going forward. You get to decide how much energy you're willing to spend on repair, and when it's time to redirect that energy toward people who see you clearly and love you well. The anger will tell you when you're ready. Trust it. It's been waiting a long time to be heard. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on sibling rivalry as adult resentment, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
